description:
You are acting as a junior log-analyst who has just been asked to produce a tiny disk-usage snapshot of a log directory so that another application can ingest the numbers automatically.

Existing material  
• A directory already exists at /home/user/logs.  
• Inside that directory are exactly three plain-text log files:
  1. access.log  
  2. error.log  
  3. app.log

What you must do  
1. From the command line, determine for *only* the three files listed above (and nothing else):
   • the number of regular files that match the names given (this will always be “3”),  
   • the cumulative byte-size of those three files taken together,  
   • the single largest file name, and  
   • the size in bytes of that largest file.

2. Create a brand-new ASCII text file named usage_summary.log **directly inside** /home/user/logs (full path: /home/user/logs/usage_summary.log).

3. Write the results to that file in the *exact* four-line key=value format shown below.  
   • There must be no leading or trailing spaces.  
   • Every key must appear in the order shown.  
   • Keys are all lower-case.  
   • Values are expressed in raw bytes (no KB/MB suffixes).  
   • End each line with a single UNIX newline (“\n”).

Required format (example only – the numbers shown below are *not* necessarily correct; generate the real ones yourself):

files=3  
bytes=XXXXX  
largest_file=YYYYY  
largest_bytes=ZZZZZ  

4. Do not write anything else to that file.

Verification  
Automated tests will:
• Confirm that /home/user/logs/usage_summary.log exists.  
• Confirm it contains exactly four lines.  
• Parse the key=value pairs and compare them against the real figures on disk.

Hints (optional – you may choose your own commands):  
• “wc -c”, “stat --printf=%s”, “du -b”, “ls -l” can all show file sizes.  
• Use sort to discover the largest file.  

End goal  
When you are done, /home/user/logs/usage_summary.log must be present and must strictly follow the required four-line format with correct numbers.
truth:
Initial setup before the agent runs
-----------------------------------
Directory structure:
  /home/user/logs/

Files and their exact contents (all plain ASCII, each logical line ends with “\n”):

1. /home/user/logs/access.log  
   • Contains exactly 100 lines.  
   • Each line is literally the five-byte string “line\n”.  
   • Therefore: size = 5 bytes × 100 = 500 bytes.

2. /home/user/logs/error.log  
   • Contains exactly 50 lines.  
   • Each line is exactly “line\n”.  
   • Therefore: size = 5 bytes × 50 = 250 bytes.

3. /home/user/logs/app.log  
   • Contains exactly 200 lines.  
   • Each line is exactly “line\n”.  
   • Therefore: size = 5 bytes × 200 = 1 000 bytes.

These three files are the *only* regular files inside /home/user/logs at start-time.

Expected state after successful task completion
-----------------------------------------------
A new file must exist:
  /home/user/logs/usage_summary.log

Its contents must be *exactly*:

files=3
bytes=1750
largest_file=app.log
largest_bytes=1000

(Each of the four lines ends with a single “\n”; there is no extra blank line at the end).

Automated verification procedure
--------------------------------
1. Read /home/user/logs/usage_summary.log.
2. Ensure file contains exactly 4 newline-terminated lines.
3. Split each line at “=” and match keys in order: files, bytes, largest_file, largest_bytes.
4. Verify values:
   – files must equal “3”.  
   – bytes must equal “1750”.  
   – largest_file must equal “app.log”.  
   – largest_bytes must equal “1000”.
